locked_facts: Work done by gas: W = ∫PdV = area under PV curve
Isothermal: W = nRT ln(V₂/V₁) — curved path on PV diagram
Isochoric: W = 0 — vertical line (no volume change)
Adiabatic: W = (P₁V₁ - P₂V₂)/(γ-1) — steeper than isothermal
Work depends on the path — it is NOT a state function
Cyclic process: net work = area enclosed by the cycle on PV diagram
truth_statements: The work done by a gas in a thermodynamic process equals the area under the PV curve
Work is path-dependent — the same initial and final states can give different work for different processes
In a cyclic process the net work done equals the area enclosed by the cycle on the PV diagram
An adiabatic curve is steeper than an isothermal curve on a PV diagram because γ > 1
In an isochoric process no work is done because the volume does not change

aha_moment.what_to_show: Two different paths between the same initial and final states on a PV diagram — one gives larger area under the curve (more work), the other gives less. The start and end points are identical, but the work is different. Work depends on the PATH, not just endpoints.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Two shaded areas under different paths — same endpoints, visibly different areas = different work
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students treat work like a state function — seeing different work for the same endpoints proves it depends on the path taken

simulation_states.label: PV diagram basics
simulation_states.description: A gas in a piston-cylinder. PV axes shown. As gas expands (V increases) at constant P, work W = PΔV = area of rectangle under the curve.
simulation_states.emphasis: Work = area under the curve on a PV diagram

simulation_states.label: Four processes
simulation_states.description: Four thermodynamic processes shown: isobaric (horizontal), isochoric (vertical, W=0), isothermal (hyperbola), adiabatic (steeper curve). Work for each calculated.
simulation_states.emphasis: Different processes = different PV curves = different work amounts

simulation_states.label: Path dependence
simulation_states.description: Two paths between same states: path 1 (isobaric then isochoric), path 2 (isochoric then isobaric). Different shaded areas = different work. Work is path-dependent.
simulation_states.emphasis: Same start and end, different paths → different work. Work is NOT a state function.

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student draws PV paths by choosing process type. Area (work) calculated automatically. Build a cycle — net work = enclosed area. Compare isothermal vs adiabatic expansion.
simulation_states.emphasis: Draw your own PV path — the area IS the work. Build a cycle and find net work.

common_misconceptions.belief: Work done depends only on initial and final states
common_misconceptions.correction: Work is a path function — it depends on the specific process (path on PV diagram), not just the initial and final states. Two different paths between the same states give different work.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show two paths between same states with different shaded areas

common_misconceptions.belief: The adiabatic and isothermal curves look the same
common_misconceptions.correction: Adiabatic curves are steeper than isothermal curves because PVᵞ = constant (γ > 1) vs PV = constant. The adiabatic process changes temperature, making pressure drop faster during expansion.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Overlay adiabatic and isothermal curves from the same starting point — adiabatic is visibly steeper
